segments.paradiso-27-g1: 'Al' is the contraction of the preposition a + the article il, meaning "to the"; here it introduces the addressee. 'Padre' is capitalized and functions as the divine title "Father."
segments.paradiso-27-g2: 'al' again = a + il, "to the." 'Figlio' is the title "Son" (here the divine Son); the comma separates coordinate items in the liturgical formula.
segments.paradiso-27-g3: Here 'a' = "to," followed by the full article 'lo' (the form used before certain masculine nouns) and the compound name 'Spirito Santo' = "Holy Spirit." The phrase completes the Trinitarian address.
segments.paradiso-27-g4: The verb 'cominciò' is the passato remoto (third person singular) of cominciare, "he began"; it narrates the action of starting the utterance that follows.
segments.paradiso-27-g5: 'gloria' is an exclamation (noun used as a vocative/exclamatory line) meaning "glory" or "praise"; here it is quoted as the content of what was begun.
segments.paradiso-27-g6: This segment is a closing quotation mark and comma; it separates the quoted words from the following narrative comment.
segments.paradiso-27-g7: 'tutto' precedes the elided article '’l' (= il) to mean "the whole" or "all of." 'paradiso' is "Paradise/Heaven." The adjective + elided article + noun order is emphatic and typical in poetry.
segments.paradiso-27-g8: 'sì che' functions as a result conjunction "so that/so". 'm’' is the clitic mi (direct or indirect object) placed before the verb; 'inebrïava' is the imperfect tense (third singular) of inebriare, "was intoxicating/overwhelming". 'Il dolce canto' is the subject (the sweet song) causing the effect. The diaeresis on ï (inebrïava) marks syllable division for pronunciation.
segments.paradiso-27-g9: 'Ciò ch’io vedeva' is a relative clause meaning "that which I saw" (here 'ch’' stands for che = "that"). 'mi sembiava' is the imperfect "seemed to me," with the clitic 'mi' marking the experiencer. 'un riso de l’universo' = "a smile/laugh of the universe," where 'de' is a poetic form of di ('of').
segments.paradiso-27-g10: 'per che' here introduces the consequence/wherefore ("therefore/so"). 'mia ebbrezza' = "my rapture/intoxication" with the possessive before the noun. 'intrava' is the imperfect of intrare, "entered/penetrated," used here to express an ongoing or recurring action. 'per l’udire e per lo viso' means "through hearing and through sight/face," using per + article for the instrumental sense; 'lo' before 'viso' is a poetic/metrical or archaic article choice.
segments.paradiso-27-g11: An exclamatory interjection: 'Oh' expresses strong feeling and 'gioia' is the noun "joy/delight." The two-word exclamation stands alone as an emotional outburst.
segments.paradiso-27-g12: A short exclamatory phrase: 'oh' is an interjection introducing the exclamation; 'ineffabile' is an adjective placed before the noun for emphasis (poetic word order); 'allegrezza' is the noun 'joy' with no article, a common poetic omission that heightens immediacy.
segments.paradiso-27-g13: 'oh' again marks an exclamation; 'vita' is the noun 'life' (no article); 'intègra' is an adjective meaning 'whole/intact' placed after the noun here (Italian allows flexible adjective position for poetic effect); 'd’ amore e di pace' are two coordinated prepositional complements introduced by the preposition 'di' (contracted to d’ before a vowel) linked by 'e' = 'and', specifying the qualities in which the life is whole.
segments.paradiso-27-g14: 'oh' is the exclamatory particle; 'sanza' is the archaic form of senza 'without' and governs the noun that follows; 'brama' here functions as a noun 'longing/desire' (poetic use without an article); 'sicura' is an adjective 'secure/certain' qualifying 'ricchezza' and placed before the noun for emphasis; 'ricchezza' is the noun 'wealth' or 'richness'. The whole phrase is an addressed exclamation with inverted, emphatic word order.
segments.paradiso-27-g15: Prepositional phrase 'Dinanzi a li occhi miei' = 'before my eyes' (Dinanzi + a + the old-form article 'li'); 'le quattro face' is the subject 'the four torches/fires'; 'stavano' is the imperfect of stare used as a copula ('were standing/being'); 'accese' is a past participle/adjective 'lit, kindled' describing their state. The imperfect 'stavano accese' gives a continuous past scene.
segments.paradiso-27-g16: Coordinating 'e' = 'and'; 'quella che pria venne' is a relative clause 'the one that came earlier/first' ('pria' = earlier/previous); 'incominciò a farsi' uses incominciare + a + reflexive 'farsi' (here 'farsi' = 'to become/make oneself') in the passato remoto ('incominciò' = 'began') so 'began to become'; 'più vivace' is the degree modifier 'more lively'. Note: 'farsi' is reflexive and here functions as 'to become', a usage not listed in the provided senses.
segments.paradiso-27-g17: 'e tal ne la sembianza sua divenne' = 'and so in its appearance it became': 'tal' = 'such/so'; 'ne' is an adverbial pronoun meaning 'in/within' here (archaic placement before the noun): 'ne la sembianza sua' = 'in its appearance'; 'divenne' is the passato remoto of 'divenire' = 'became'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect.
segments.paradiso-27-g18: 'qual diverrebbe Iove,' is a comparative clause introduced by 'qual' meaning 'as'/'such as'; 'diverrebbe' is the conditional ('would become') of an older verb form meaning 'become'; 'Iove' is the poetic name for Jupiter. Together it means 'such as Jupiter would become...'.
segments.paradiso-27-g19: 's’elli e Marte fossero augelli e cambiassersi penne.' is a protasis (if-clause) in older style: 's’' contracts 'se' ('if') and 'elli' refers back to Iove ('he'); 'fossero' is the imperfect subjunctive ('were') used for a hypothetical; 'augelli' = 'birds'; 'cambiassersi' is a reflexive imperfect-subjunctive form 'change themselves' (here 'change their feathers'); 'penne' = 'feathers'. The sequence expresses a hypothetical comparison ('if he and Mars were birds and changed their feathers'). Note: the contraction 's’' is archaic and its provided senses do not list 'if', so the sense for it is not available.
segments.paradiso-27-g20: La provedenza = definite article + noun: 'La' is the feminine singular definite article 'the', and 'provedenza' (archaic spelling of provvidenza/provenienza) is used here as a personified noun 'Providence'. This begins the sentence and will be modified by the following relative clause.
segments.paradiso-27-g21: che quivi comparte vice e officio: 'che' is a relative pronoun 'who/which' referring back to 'La provedenza'; 'quivi' is an adverb 'there/here' indicating place; 'comparte' is present indicative 3rd person singular of compartere (poetic) meaning 'apportions/assigns'; 'vice e officio' are two nominal objects in apposition (vice = viceregency or delegated authority; officio = office/post or duty) showing what Providence distributes.
segments.paradiso-27-g22: nel beato coro silenzio posto avea da ogne parte: 'nel' = in the; 'beato coro' = the blessed choir (adjective before noun); 'silenzio' is the direct object/thing placed; 'posto avea' is compound construction with past participle before the auxiliary (literary order) = 'had placed'; 'da ogne parte' = 'on/from every side' (da + partitive/adverbial phrase) specifying extent.
segments.paradiso-27-g23: quand’ ïo udi’: 'quand’' (quand + elision) = 'when'; 'ïo' (with diaeresis marking stress) is subject 'I'; 'udi’' is the archaic past perfect/preterite 'I heard' (from udire) with elision of final vowel. The clause marks the temporal point when the speech that follows was heard.
segments.paradiso-27-g24: «Se io mi trascoloro,: 'Se' = 'if' (conditional particle); 'io' subject 'I' is emphatic; 'mi' is reflexive pronoun 'myself'; 'trascoloro' is present indicative (1st singular) or poetic present used like a potential 'I change color/turn pale'. The clause gives a conditional protasis (if I blanch/change).
segments.paradiso-27-g25: non ti maravigliar,: negative imperative 'non ... maravigliar' = 'do not be amazed'; 'ti' is the second-person singular object/anticomitative pronoun 'yourself/you'. The verb appears in an imperative form without final -e (poetic/informal) directed to the listener (you).
segments.paradiso-27-g26: ché,: conjunction 'ché' = 'for/because', giving the reason for the preceding imperative; it introduces the explanatory clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-27-g27: dicend’ io,: gerund 'dicend'' (dicendo with elision) = 'saying' used adverbially, followed by the subject 'io' for emphasis. This structure ('dicend' io') means 'as I say' or 'when I say it' and links the manner of what follows to the speaker's utterance.
segments.paradiso-27-g28: vedrai trascolorar tutti costoro.: 'vedrai' is future tense 2nd person singular 'you will see'; it takes the infinitive 'trascolorar' (infinitive without final -e due to poetic elision) as complement: 'you will see [them] change color'; 'tutti costoro' = 'all these/ all of them' is the object of the infinitive and indicates who will change.
segments.paradiso-27-g29: Quelli ch’usurpa in terra il luogo mio,: 'Quelli' is a demonstrative/pronominal form used like 'he/that one' or 'those who' and is followed by a relative 'ch’'='che' = 'who'; 'usurpa' is present indicative 'usurps' (he usurps); 'in terra' = 'on earth'; 'il luogo mio' = 'my place' (direct object). The phrase names the person(s) who occupy the speaker's rightful place.
segments.paradiso-27-g30: il luogo mio,: repetition for emphasis. 'Il luogo mio' is a nominal phrase 'the place of mine' (my place) repeated as an accusative object; the anaphora stresses possession and outrage.
segments.paradiso-27-g31: il lugar mio,: third repetition (same structure) functioning as exclamatory reiteration; the pause and repetition are rhetorical, drawing attention to the violated place.
segments.paradiso-27-g32: che vaca ne la presenza del Figliuol di Dio,: 'che' = relative 'which' referring to 'il luogo mio'; 'vaca' (vacare) is present indicative meaning 'stands empty / is vacant' (archaic form); 'ne la presenza' = 'in the presence' (ne = in/within here); 'del Figliuol di Dio' = 'of the Son of God' (del = di + il). The clause explains that the place is empty even before Christ.
segments.paradiso-27-g33: fatt’ ha del cimitero mio cloaca del sangue e de la puzza;: 'fatt' (fatto, past participle) precedes the auxiliary 'ha' to form 'ha fatto' = 'has made'; word order here is inverted for poetic effect. 'del cimitero mio' = 'of my cemetery' (i.e., my burial ground); 'cloaca' = 'sewer/cesspool'; 'del sangue e de la puzza' = 'of blood and of stench' — the phrase says he has turned the burial place into a cesspool of blood and filth.
segments.paradiso-27-g34: onde ’l perverso che cadde di qua sù,: 'onde' = 'from which/therefore' introducing result or consequence; '’l perverso' = 'the depraved one/the wrongdoer' (definite article + adjective used substantively); 'che cadde' = 'who fell' (past tense cadde = fell); 'di qua sù' = 'from this side up here' (di = from; qua = here/this side; sù = up) — it locates the fall and identifies the wicked who fell from the higher place.
segments.paradiso-27-g35: là giù si placa».: 'là giù' = 'there below' (là = there; giù = down/below); 'si placa' is reflexive present indicative 3rd person singular of placare meaning 'is appeased/placated' or 'finds rest' — the reflexive 'si' indicates the subject is pacified (or finds satisfaction) there below. The phrase closes the quotation describing the consequence for the wicked.
segments.paradiso-27-g36: Di quel color = literal prepositional phrase 'of that color' (Di + demonstrative + noun) that introduces the quality being described. che introduces a relative clause 'which ...' describing that color. per lo sole avverso is a circumstantial phrase 'by/through the sun being opposite' (per = by/through; lo sole = the sun; avverso = 'opposed' or 'opposite' describing the sun); the adjective follows the noun in poetic word order. nube dipigne is the verb clause with the subject 'nube' (cloud) followed by the poetical present 'dipigne' (dipinge) 'paints' or 'stains'; da sera e da mane means 'at evening and at morning' (da used with times = 'at' or 'from'). Together the relative clause says that the cloud is painted that color at evening and morning because of the sun's opposition.
segments.paradiso-27-g37: vid’ ïo allora tutto ’l ciel cosperso is an inverted, compressed past-tense statement. vid’ (elided form of vidi) is the first-person past 'I saw'; ïo emphasizes the subject 'I' (redundant for emphasis). allora = 'then'; tutto ’l ciel = 'the whole heaven/sky' (tutto modifying the definite phrase); cosperso is a past participle/adjectival perfect meaning 'sprinkled' or 'studded' and functions as the complement of what was seen.
segments.paradiso-27-g38: E come donna onesta che permane di sé sicura is a simile introduced by 'E come' = 'And like/as'. donna onesta = 'an honourable/decent woman'; che permane = relative clause 'who remains' (permane is present indicative 'remains'); di sé sicura is a reflexive prepositional phrase 'secure/ confident in herself' (di + sé + adjective). The whole phrase sets up a comparison for Beatrice's behaviour.
segments.paradiso-27-g39: e per l’altrui fallanza is a short concessive/explanatory phrase: e = 'and'; per = 'because of' or 'on account of' here; l’altrui = 'that of others' / 'others''; fallanza = 'failure, fault, or failing'. Together it says that, because of others' failing, the woman's steadiness is compromised.
segments.paradiso-27-g40: pur ascoltando uses pur as an adverb 'even/although/while' giving the nuance 'even while' or 'though while'; ascoltando is the present participle/gerund 'listening' (literally 'while listening'), so the phrase indicates simultaneous action.
segments.paradiso-27-g41: timida si fane is an idiomatic, compact clause: timida = 'timid' or 'faint'; si is the reflexive pronoun used with the verb; fane is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'becomes' (here conveying 'she becomes timid'). Word order (adjective before verb) gives the effect of immediate change.
segments.paradiso-27-g42: così Beatrice trasmutò sembianza; cosí = 'thus' or 'so'; Beatrice is the subject; trasmutò is the perfect-past 'changed' (trasmutare); sembianza = 'appearance' or 'countenance'. The clause states the result of the preceding simile and actions: Beatrice changed her look.
segments.paradiso-27-g43: e tale eclissi credo che ’n ciel fue quando patì la supprema possanza is a reportive clause: e tale eclissi = 'and such an eclipse'; credo = 'I believe' (present tense used to introduce Dante's opinion); che introduces subordinate clause; ’n ciel fue = 'there was in heaven' (fue = past 'was'); quando patì la supprema possanza = temporal clause 'when the supreme Power suffered' (patì = past 'suffered' or 'endured', la supprema possanza = 'the supreme power').
segments.paradiso-27-g138: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' and sets the manner. si fa is a reflexive/impersonal construction (si + fare, 3rd singular) that here means 'becomes' or 'is made' rather than a literal 'makes'; it yields a passive-like sense ('the skin becomes'). la pelle is the noun phrase 'the skin.' The pair bianca nera places two adjectives in immediate juxtaposition (poetic compression) to indicate a change/contrast — 'white, then black' or alternating pallor/darkness. nel primo aspetto means 'at the first appearance/first aspect' (aspetto = appearance/phase), with nel = in + the. de la bella figlia uses the archaic preposition de = di (of) to form 'of the fair daughter'; di quel ch’... begins a relative clause: quel = 'that' and ch’ is the clitic form of che = 'who/which,' introducing 'who brings morning and leaves evening' (apporta, lascia are present indicative 3rd singular describing the habitual/defining action).
segments.paradiso-27-g139: The pronoun 'Tu' is a direct vocative address to the reader or listener, placed at the start for emphasis; it functions as the person being spoken to and is not grammatically linked to a verb in this segment.
segments.paradiso-27-g140: 'perché' here introduces a purpose/result clause meaning 'so that' or 'lest'; it governs a verb in the subjunctive. 'non' negates the clause. 'ti' is the dative clitic meaning 'to you' placed before the verb. 'facci' is an archaic/poetic present subjunctive form (Dante's -i ending) corresponding to modern Italian 'faccia' (3rd person singular), meaning 'make' or 'cause'. 'maraviglia' is a noun object/predicative that follows the verb: 'cause (you) wonder'.
segments.paradiso-27-g141: 'pensa' is a 2nd-person singular imperative (or hortative present) meaning 'consider' or 'think' and addresses the same 'Tu'. 'che' introduces the content clause. '’n' is a poetic contraction of 'in' (’n terra = in terra = 'on earth'). 'terra' here means 'the world/earth'. The clause 'non è chi governi' literally 'there is not anyone who governs' uses 'non è' (there is not) plus a relative/expletive 'chi' and the verb 'governi' in the present subjunctive (Dante's subjunctive form) to express the indefiniteness/absence of a ruling person.
segments.paradiso-27-g142: 'onde' is a connective adverb/conjunction meaning 'whence' or 'therefore' (introducing the result of the preceding statement). 'sì' is an adverb meaning 'so' or 'thus' and intensifies the following verb. 'svïa' is the present indicative 3rd-person singular of 'sviare' (the diaeresis on ï shows vowel separation in the poem) meaning 'leads astray' or 'sends astray'. The subject 'l’umana famiglia' ('the human family' = humanity) follows the verb (V–S order), yielding 'hence the human family is so led astray.'
segments.paradiso-27-g143: Ma prima che ... si sverni is introduced by the temporal phrase 'prima che' which requires the subjunctive in Italian; here 'si sverni' is a reflexive/pronominal verb form (si + svernare) in the present subjunctive (third person singular) meaning 'before ... passes/undergoes the winter.' 'Tutto' precedes the verb to emphasize completeness ('entirely' or 'completely'). 'Per' here gives the reason or cause ('on account of' / 'because of') introducing 'la centesma' (the noun with definite article). The relative clause 'ch’è là giù negletta' is the compressed form of 'che è'; 'là giù' = 'down there' and 'negletta' is a past-participial adjective 'neglected'.
segments.paradiso-27-g144: The verb 'raggeran' is a future form (poetic spelling without the doubled -nn-) meaning 'they will shine/gleam.' It appears before the subject as a stylistic inversion typical of poetry. 'Sì' here is an adverb meaning 'so' (introducing the consequence/result), and 'questi cerchi superni' is the subject phrase 'these heavenly circles' (cerchi = 'circles', superni = 'heavenly/celestial').
segments.paradiso-27-g145: The initial 'che' introduces a clause of result/explanation linked to the previous line. 'La fortuna' is the noun 'the fortune/fate' and the following 'che tanto s’aspetta' is a relative clause modifying it: 'che' = 'which/that', 'tanto' = 'so much/so greatly', and 's'aspetta' is the pronominal construction of 'aspettare' with clitic 'si' producing an impersonal/passive sense 'is awaited' or 'is expected.' Together: 'the fortune that is so much expected.'
segments.paradiso-27-g146: This clause places the direct object 'le poppe' ('the sterns' in a nautical sense) at the front for emphasis, followed by the verb 'volgerà' (future third-person singular of volgere, 'will turn'). 'U’' is a poetic contraction of 'ove' = 'where,' and 'son' is the contracted form of 'sono' = 'are'; 'le prore' = 'the prows.' Literally: 'it/that will turn the sterns to where the prows are.'
segments.paradiso-27-g147: 'Sì che' here functions as a result conjunction 'so that' introducing the consequence. 'La classe' means 'the class/group' (in nautical context, a formation or fleet of ships) and is the subject of 'correrà' (future of 'correre' used here as 'will run/sail/pass'). 'Diretta' is a past participle used adjectivally to mean 'direct' or 'straight'—so: 'so that the fleet will run straight.'
segments.paradiso-27-g148: A closing, balanced clause: 'e' = 'and', 'vero' = 'true/real', 'frutto' = 'fruit/result.' 'Verrà' is the future of 'venire' ('will come') and 'dopo 'l fiore' = 'after the flower' (’l is the elided article il). Figuratively, the phrase says a genuine result will follow the blossom—the expected outcome will follow the promising sign.
segments.paradiso-27-g63: Ma is the coordinating conjunction 'but' introducing contrast. L’alta provedenza is a noun phrase with the definite article contracted (l’) before the adjective alta + noun provedenza; word order (article + adjective + noun) is poetic but normal in Italian. The whole phrase names the acting subject of the following clauses.
segments.paradiso-27-g64: Che is a relative pronoun ('who/which') referring back to provedenza. Con Scipio is a prepositional phrase ('with Scipio') marking accompaniment. Difese is a past (perfect) tense verb (he/she/it defended); a Roma uses the preposition a to indicate location ('in/at Rome'), and la gloria del mondo is the direct object ('the glory of the world'). The clause is a relative clause giving what Providence did.
segments.paradiso-27-g65: Soccorrà is a future tense verb (3rd person singular) meaning 'will help/come to aid' — an archaic or poetic form related to soccorrere. Tosto is an adverb 'soon/quickly' modifying that future.
segments.paradiso-27-g66: Sì com’ io concipio is an adverbial simile 'as (or just) as I conceive/think': sì com’ (= sì come, the elided form com’) introduces a comparison, io is the subject, concipio is present tense 'I conceive/think'.
segments.paradiso-27-g67: E tu is simply 'and you' addressing the addressee; e coordinates this clause with the previous assertion.
segments.paradiso-27-g68: Figliuol is a vocative (address) 'son' — an archaic/poetic spelling of figliuolo; it stands alone to call the listener.
segments.paradiso-27-g69: Che introduces a subordinate relative clause modifying 'tu' ('who'). Per lo mortal pondo is an old-fashioned noun phrase: per + article + adjective + noun, here meaning 'through/by the mortal weight' (i.e. the burden/realm of mortality). Ancor giù tornerai is future indicative 'you will still return down' (ancor = yet/still, giù = down), so the clause says he will descend again into mortal life.
segments.paradiso-27-g70: Apri is an imperative (2nd person singular) 'open'; la bocca is the direct object 'the mouth' (article + noun). The clause is a direct command addressed to the 'son'.
segments.paradiso-27-g71: E non asconder is another imperative construction with the negative non + infinitive asconder 'do not hide'. Quel ch’io non ascondo is a nominal clause 'that which I do not hide' (quel = 'that', ch’ = che = 'that/which', io = subject 'I', non + ascondo = 'I do not hide'). Note ascondo in this edition is a poetic verb form; the speaker contrasts his own non-concealment with the addressee's.
segments.paradiso-27-g72: Sì come introduces a simile 'just as'. Di vapor gelati is a noun phrase 'of frozen vapours' (di + noun + adjective). Fiocca is an impersonal/present verb 'it flakes / falls in flakes' (poetic use), and in giuso l’aere nostro places the action 'into our lower air' (in giuso = downward, l’aere nostro = 'our air/atmosphere').
segments.paradiso-27-g73: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when'. ’L corno de la capra del ciel is a genitive chain 'the horn of the goat of the sky/heaven' (corno = horn). Col sol si tocca is a short clause 'with the sun it touches / the sun reaches it' where col is con + il, sol is 'sun' (here the celestial sun), si is the reflexive/passive particle used with tocca 'touches/reaches'. This is astronomical/astrological imagery (zodiacal goat).
segments.paradiso-27-g74: In sù vid’ io così is inverted, poetic word order: in sù 'upwards' + vid’ (elided vidi) 'I saw' + io 'I' for emphasis + così 'thus'. L’etera addorno farsi e fioccar uses l’etera (the ether/upper sky) + addorno (adorned) with verbs farsi (reflexive/become/made) and fioccar (to snow/flake) in an infinitival/verb cluster meaning 'be made and to flake'. Di vapor trïunfanti is a prepositional noun phrase 'with triumphant vapours' and che fatto avien con noi quivi soggiorno is a relative clause 'which had made a stay there with us' (fatto + avien = past perfect construction 'had made').
segments.paradiso-27-g75: Lo viso mio seguiva i suoi sembianti is a simple past-continuous description: Lo viso mio = 'my face' (lo used poetically as the article); seguiva = imperfect 'followed' or 'kept following' (ongoing past action); i suoi sembianti = 'her expressions/semblances' (direct object). It means Dante's gaze kept following her face.
segments.paradiso-27-g76: e seguì fin che ’l mezzo, e = 'and'; seguì = perfect/past 'followed' (here continuing the motion of the gaze); fin che = 'until'; ’l mezzo = 'the middle' (i.e. until the midpoint). The clause marks how long the following continued.
segments.paradiso-27-g77: per lo molto is an ablative/causal phrase: per = 'because of' or 'on account of'; lo molto = 'the great number/abundance' (molto here used nominally). It gives the reason why what follows happens.
segments.paradiso-27-g78: li tolse il trapassar del più avanti: li is the clitic object pronoun referring back to the things being followed (i.e. the visive points); tolse = past 'took away' or 'removed'; il trapassar = 'the passing' (action of passing by); del più avanti = 'of the one more forward/ahead' (più avanti = 'further ahead'). The whole clause says that the forward movement removed them from his view.
segments.paradiso-27-g79: 'Onde' at the start here functions as 'so/therefore', introducing the result or immediate action; 'la donna' is the definite subject phrase (article + noun) = 'the lady' (the woman who speaks).
segments.paradiso-27-g80: This is a relative clause introduced by 'che' = 'who/that'. 'mi' is the indirect object 'me'; 'vide' is passato remoto (3rd sg. of vedere) meaning 'saw'; 'assolto' is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'freed/absolved' and complements 'mi vide' ('saw me freed'). 'de l’attendere in sù' = 'from (the) waiting/upward looking', where 'de' = 'from/of' and 'l'attendere' is the infinitive used as a noun (the act of waiting), with 'in sù' = 'upward'.
segments.paradiso-27-g81: 'mi disse' is simple: 'disse' is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of dire = 'said', and the clitic 'mi' marks the indirect object 'to me'; the comma and colon that follow introduce the direct speech that comes next.
segments.paradiso-27-g82: The speech begins with 'Adima', an exclamatory/imperative particle used to call attention (introducing the command). 'Il viso' is the direct object 'the face' (i.e. 'your face') of the first imperative; 'e guarda' links a second imperative 'guarda' (2nd sg. command 'look'). In the subordinate clause 'come tu se' vòlto', 'come' = 'how', 'tu' is the explicit subject pronoun for emphasis, 'se' is an archaic/poetic contracted form of 'sei' (you are), and 'vòlto' is a past participle used adjectivally = 'turned/facing'.
segments.paradiso-27-g100: Le parti sue is the subject: the feminine plural noun parti ("parts") with the possessive sue ("its/her/their") modified by two coordinating adjectives vivissime (superlative/adverbial in form: "most/lively/very alive") and eccelse ("exalted/sublime"). Sì uniforme son places the adverb sì before the predicate adjective uniforme and uses son (poetic/contracted plural of sono) as the 3rd-person plural present of essere: “are so uniform.”
segments.paradiso-27-g101: Ch' introduces the result clause (sì ... ch' = "so ... that"). i' is the poetic contraction of io ("I"); non so dire is the present tense of sapere + infinitive ("I do not know how to say" / "I cannot say"). Qual Bëatrice per loco mi scelse: qual = "which/what (one)"; per loco = "by/according to place" (Dante means "from which position/sphere"); scelse is passato remoto (3rd-singular of scegliere) meaning "chose (me)".
segments.paradiso-27-g102: Ma = "but." Ella is the nominative feminine pronoun "she" (subject of the following relative clause and verbs); the comma isolates her as the topic/agent of the action that follows.
segments.paradiso-27-g103: Che is the relative pronoun "who/that" introducing the clause modifying ella. Vedëa is the imperfect tense of vedere ("she saw / perceived / was seeing"), here conveying that she perceived Dante's longing; l' is the article introducing mio disire ("my desire/longing"). Note the diaeresis in vedëa indicating the vowel break (ve-dë-a).
segments.paradiso-27-g104: Incominciò is the passato remoto (3rd-singular) of incominciare: "she began." In narrative/poetic style Dante often uses passato remoto to report a single completed action launching direct speech.
segments.paradiso-27-g105: Ridendo is a gerund ("laughing") that expresses the manner of incominciò; tanto ("so/very") modifies the adjective lieta (feminine, agreeing with ella) — "laughing so happily." The phrase indicates simultaneous action and the joyful tone.
segments.paradiso-27-g106: Che here introduces a result clause: "so ... that." Dio parea uses pare(a)re in the imperfect ("seemed") with the infinitive gioire as its complement: "God seemed to rejoice." Nel suo volto is a locative phrase "in her face," where suo agrees with ella.
segments.paradiso-27-g107: This begins the direct speech with a nominative phrase: La natura del mondo = "The nature of the world." The article + noun + genitive (del mondo) names the subject of the explanation that follows.
segments.paradiso-27-g108: Che is a relative pronoun referring back to "nature" ("which"). Quïeta is 3rd-singular present of quïetare ("calms/quiet[s]"); the diaeresis marks the syllable division. Il mezzo = "the middle/center," and tutto l'altro intorno = "all the rest around it;" move is 3rd-singular present of muovere ("moves/sets in motion"), so the clause means that the world's nature calms the center and moves everything else around it.
segments.paradiso-27-g109: Quinci is an adverbial particle meaning "from here / hence." Comincia is present tense "begins" and come da sua meta = "as if from its goal/limit" (meta = turning-point or boundary). The whole clause: "it begins here as from its limit."
segments.paradiso-27-g110: E questo cielo non ha altro dove che ... is a restrictive formula: "and this heaven has no other where (place) than ..." Non ha = "does not have"; altro dove = "any other place/where," che introduces the exclusive identification of that place (la mente divina).
segments.paradiso-27-g111: In che = "in which" (relative to la mente divina). S'accende is the pronominal/intransitive form of accendere (here passive/inchoative sense "is kindled/ignited"); the subject of accende is l'amor ("the love") which the relative clause then qualifies: che 'l volge = "that turns it" (volge = turns/causes to turn) and la virtù ch'ei piove = "the virtue which he (ei = he/God) showers/pours (down)." Piove (to rain) is used metaphorically: God 'rains down' virtue.
segments.paradiso-27-g112: Luce e amor = "light and love" (compound abstract subject). D'un cerchio (= di un cerchio) indicates they belong to or form "one circle" (i.e. in a single ring). Lui is the dative/accusative pronoun "him," and comprende is 3rd-singular present of comprendere ("encompasses/includes"). Poetically the pair luce e amor is treated as the content of a single circle that encompasses him.
segments.paradiso-27-g113: Sì come = "just as." Questo refers back to the current circle; li altri = "the others" (archaic li = the) — the verb from the previous clause (comprende) is omitted by ellipsis, so the line reads "just as this [one does to] the others."
segments.paradiso-27-g114: E quel precinto = "and that girdle/enclosure/band." Colui che 'l cinge = "he who encircles it" (colui = 'the one/person who'), with che as the relative pronoun. Solamente intende = "only understands/grasps." The word order places the object (quel precinto) before its subject for emphasis: only the encircler truly understands the enclosing band.
segments.paradiso-27-g115: Non è is the negative copula (non + è) meaning 'is not.' 'suo moto' is a possessive adjective + noun ('its motion'). 'per altro distinto' places the adjective 'distinto' (past participle used adjectivally, 'distinguished/separated') after the modifying phrase 'per altro' ('by/for any other'), a slightly inverted/poetic order meaning 'distinguished by any other thing.'
segments.paradiso-27-g116: ma ('but') introduces the contrast. 'li altri' uses the archaic definite article li + 'altri' = 'the others.' 'son' is the contracted/archaic present indicative of 'sono' (they are). 'mensurati' is a past participle used passively ('measured'/'meted out'), and 'da questo' indicates the standard or cause ('by/according to this').
segments.paradiso-27-g117: sì come is a comparative/illustrative phrase 'just as' or 'as.' diece here is an archaic/poetic form for the number ten (elliptical: 'as ten [is measured]'), and the following 'da mezzo e da quinto' uses da to express relation or measure ('by/from the middle and by/from the fifth'). The clause is elliptical: the verb 'are measured' from the previous line is understood to apply to diece.
segments.paradiso-27-g118: This is a simile introduced by 'e come' ('and as'). 'il tempo' is the subject ('time'). 'tegna' is an archaic/poetic present form of 'tenere' meaning 'holds'/'keeps' (here best read as 'plants/places/keeps'); it takes as complement 'in cotal testo le sue radici' ('in such an arrangement/text its roots'). The second half parallels the first with a pronominal 'ne' ('in/of them') + 'li altri' ('the others') followed by 'le fronde' ('the branches/leaves'), so the structure contrasts roots placed in this scheme and branches in the other schemes.
segments.paradiso-27-g119: omai is an adverb meaning 'by now/now.' 'a te' marks the person affected ('to you'). 'può esser' is the modal periphrasis (potere + infinitive 'esser') meaning 'can be' or 'may be,' and 'manifesto' is a predicate adjective 'manifest/evident.' The whole phrase means 'it can now be made manifest to you.'
segments.paradiso-27-g120: Oh (vocative interjection) + noun cupidigia (covetousness) is the grammatical subject of the following relative clause introduced by che. I is the article 'i mortali' (the mortals) and affonde is a present indicative verb meaning 'drowns/sinks' with sì + sotto te giving the degree/location 'so far under you.'
segments.paradiso-27-g121: The second che introduces the result/consequence clause: 'that no one has the power to...'. Nessuno is the negative subject, ha is present indicative of avere, podere is the noun 'power', and di + infinitive (trarre) marks the complement 'to pull out.' Fuor de le tue onde is a prepositional phrase 'out of your waves.'
segments.paradiso-27-g122: Ben (adverb 'well') modifies fiorisce, a present indicative 'blossoms/flourishes.' Ne li uomini is an old-fashioned prepositional phrase meaning 'in men' (in human beings), and il volere is the subject noun phrase 'the will' placed after the verb for stylistic inversion.
segments.paradiso-27-g123: Ma introduces contrast ('but'). La pioggia continüa is a noun modified by what appears as a participial/adjectival form meaning 'continuous rain.' Converte is present indicative 'turns/transforms,' in marks the result 'into' and bozzacchioni is the resulting coarse lumps; le sosine vere ('the true little shoots') is the direct object of the transformation.
segments.paradiso-27-g124: Fede e innocenza are the coordinated subjects. Son is the contracted form of sono (present indicative 'are'), and reperte is a past participle used adjectivally/predicatively 'found' (son reperte = 'are found'). Solo ne' parvoletti means 'only in the little ones/children' (ne = in).
segments.paradiso-27-g125: Poi 'afterwards' sets the temporal sequence. Ciascuna 'each one' is feminine and is the subject of pria fugge: pria (archaic) = 'before/soon' and fugge is present indicative 'flees.' Che introduces the subordinate clause expressing what is avoided: 'before the cheeks are covered' where sian is the subjunctive plural (siano) used after a temporal/anticipatory expression, and coperte is the past participle 'covered.'
segments.paradiso-27-g126: Tale is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'such (a one)' or 'such a child' and introduces an example; the comma shows the phrase is resumed by participial clauses that follow.
segments.paradiso-27-g127: Balbuzïendo is a present participle/gerund 'stammering' describing the subject 'tale'; ancor (ancora) is an adverb 'still' modifying the participle to mean 'still stammering.'
segments.paradiso-27-g128: Digiuna is present indicative 'he fasts' (or 'is fasting'), a simple statement about the same subject 'tale'; the comma links it to the following contrasting action.
segments.paradiso-27-g129: Che poi divora: che introduces a following action (here explicative/consecutive), poi 'then/afterwards' gives the sequence, and divora is present indicative 'devours' (he who earlier fasted later devours).
segments.paradiso-27-g130: Con la lingua sciolta is an instrumental phrase 'with the tongue loosened' (con + noun phrase). Lingua here functions metaphorically as 'speech/tongue' and sciolta is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'loosened' or 'freed' (i.e. speaking fluently).
segments.paradiso-27-g131: Qualunque cibo 'whatever food' is the direct object of divora; per qualunque luna is an idiomatic temporal phrase 'under/at any moon' (i.e. at any time/under any lunar phase), per marking the temporal scope.
segments.paradiso-27-g132: E tal: and another such (e = and, tal = such), introducing the second example parallel to the first; tal repeats 'tale' in abbreviated form.
segments.paradiso-27-g133: Balbuzïendo again is the participle 'stammering,' here modifying the new 'tal' subject; the participial phrase is repeated to show the same defect appears in the second type of child.
segments.paradiso-27-g134: Ama e ascolta are two coordinated present verbs 'loves and listens to/obeys' with la madre sua as their object 'his mother.' The coordinate verbs describe the child's affectionate and obedient attitude.
segments.paradiso-27-g135: Che is the relative pronoun introducing the subordinate clause that describes the mother (who does something later).
segments.paradiso-27-g136: Con loquela intera: con + noun phrase means 'with full speech/delivery'; loquela 'speech/diction' and intera 'whole/complete' together indicate the mother, unlike her stammering child, speaks fully and plainly.
segments.paradiso-27-g137: Disïa is an archaic imperfect (or a past-tense form) meaning 'she desired/wished'; poi 'then/afterwards' marks time, di + infinitive vederla 'to see her' is the verbal complement, and sepolta is the past participle 'buried' describing what she wished for (to see her buried).
segments.paradiso-27-g44: Poi is an adverb meaning 'then/afterwards.' procedetter is an archaic past tense form (passato remoto) of procedere, here 3rd person plural meaning 'proceeded/continued.' le parole sue places the possessive after the noun (typical in older Italian) and means 'her words' (parole = utterances). con voce tanto da sé trasmutata is an adjunct describing the manner: con + noun ('with a voice') plus tanto ... da to introduce the degree/result relationship ('so... that'); da sé ('from herself' / 'from her usual self') and trasmutata is a past participle agreeing with voce, meaning 'transformed/altered.'
segments.paradiso-27-g45: che introduces the result clause linked to tanto ... da ('so... that'): 'such that.' la sembianza is the subject 'the appearance/likeness.' non si mutò piùe is negative + reflexive verb mutare in passato remoto (mutò) with si (reflexive) meaning 'changed itself' or 'was changed'; piùe is an archaic spelling of più ('anymore/any longer').
segments.paradiso-27-g46: Non fu is the negative plus the passato remoto of essere ('was not'). la sposa di Cristo is the subject phrase 'the bride of Christ' (i.e. the Church). allevata is a past participle agreeing with sposa (feminine) and used passively: 'reared/ brought up.' del sangue mio = di + il + sangue mio: 'from/my blood' or 'by my blood.'
segments.paradiso-27-g47: di is the preposition 'of/from.' Lin here refers to Linus (a papal name) in context; the printed senses offered ('linen/flax') do not match the use as a proper name, so the available senses are inapplicable.
segments.paradiso-27-g48: di quel di Cleto is a compressed way to say 'from that of Cletus'—quel ('that') points back to the blood just mentioned, and Cleto is the proper name (Pope Cletus). The repeated di marks possession/origin.
segments.paradiso-27-g49: per essere introduces purpose with an infinitive: 'to be' or 'in order to be.' ad acquisto d'oro usata means 'used for the acquisition of gold': ad + acquisto ('for the acquiring/gain'), d'oro = 'of gold,' and usata (past participle, feminine) describes the sposa—'used' or 'employed' for that purpose. The participle at the end is typical poetic word order.
segments.paradiso-27-g50: ma introduces the contrast 'but.' per acquisto d’esto viver lieto = 'for the gaining of this joyful life' (esto = archaic 'this,' viver = 'living/life,' lieto = 'happy/joyful'). The sequence e Sisto e Pïo e Calisto e Urbano lists popes by name; sparser is an archaic past-tense form of spargere ('they scattered/shed'), here 'they shed' lo sangue = 'the blood' meaning they shed blood, and dopo molto fleto = 'after much lamentation' (fleto = weeping/lament).
segments.paradiso-27-g51: Non fu nostra intenzion = 'It was not our intention' (fu = passato remoto of essere). ch’a destra mano d’i nostri successor parte sedesse is a subordinate clause introduced by che; sedesse is the imperfect subjunctive (or archaic subjunctive form) required by an intention/desired-state clause: 'that on the right hand of our successors part should sit.' Note the older placement of possessive and the elliptical use of part.
segments.paradiso-27-g52: This continues the distribution: 'part on the other side of the Christian people.' parte da l'altra = 'part on the other (side)'; del popol cristiano = 'of the Christian people' (genitive). da indicates position/sides here.
segments.paradiso-27-g53: né che introduces another negative clause ('nor that'). le chiavi che mi fuor concesse: chiavi = 'the keys'; che ... che are relative markers; mi = 'to me'; fuor concesse is an archaic compound corresponding to furono concesse ('were granted')—fuor here is an old form used with the past participle concesse.
segments.paradiso-27-g54: divenisser is an archaic subjunctive/conditional form of divenire ('to become') used after the negative clause: 'should become.' signaculo is a diminutive token/insignia; in vessillo = 'on a banner/standard.' che contra battezzati combattesse is a relative clause: combattesse is the imperfect subjunctive/conditional 'would fight' or 'might fight,' and contra + battezzati means 'against the baptized' (battezzati is a past participle used substantively: 'the baptized').
segments.paradiso-27-g55: né ch’io fossi figura di sigillo a privilegi venduti e mendaci: another negative subordinate: 'nor that I should be a figure (representation) of a seal for privileges sold and mendacious.' fossi is imperfect subjunctive of essere (expressing the hypothetical/not intended state). a indicates the relation 'to' or 'for' (being a sign for those privileges). venduti is past participle 'sold'; mendaci 'lying/false.'
segments.paradiso-27-g56: ond' io sovente arrosso e disfavillo: ond' (onde) = 'whence/therefore' linking resultively to the previous clause; io is explicit subject; sovente = 'often.' arrosso and disfavillo are first-person present forms meaning 'I blush/turn red' and 'I extinguish quivering sparks' (disfavillo is a rare verb meaning to quench the spark, used metaphorically). The verbs are present indicative describing Dante's reaction.
segments.paradiso-27-g57: In vesta di pastor = 'in the garment/guise of a shepherd' (vesta = robe/guise). lupi rapaci is the plural subject 'rapacious wolves' (rapaci = predatory). si veggion is a reflexive/impersonal present construction of vedere (veggion ≈ vedono/si vedono): 'are seen' or 'one sees.' di qua sù is a locative phrase 'on this side/up here,' and per tutti i paschi = 'throughout all the pastures' (per = through, paschi = pastures). Word order is compact and poetic.
segments.paradiso-27-g58: o difesa di Dio is a vocative/exclamation: 'O defense (protection) of God'—addressing divine protection or calling on God as defender. difesa = defense/protection; di Dio = 'of God.'
segments.paradiso-27-g59: perché pur giaci? is a direct question: perché = 'why'; pur (here) = 'still/yet' (an intensifier), and giaci is 2nd person singular present of giacere ('to lie'), here 'lie idle' or 'remain inactive.' The speaker asks why divine protection still lies idle.
segments.paradiso-27-g60: Del sangue nostro = 'of our blood' (indicating what will be drunk). Caorsini e Guaschi are proper names/groups (subjects). s’apparecchian is a reflexive present tense (apparecchiano) meaning 'they prepare themselves' or 'set themselves up' (s’ = si enclitic), and di bere = 'to drink' (infinitive). The phrase means they prepare to drink our blood (figuratively: to shed our blood or take revenge).
segments.paradiso-27-g61: o buon principio is a vocative/exclamation: 'O good beginning'—addressing the promising start ironically. buon is the pre-nominal form of 'good' modifying principio ('beginning/origin').
segments.paradiso-27-g62: a che vil fine convien che tu caschi! is an exclamatory question: a che = 'to what,' vil fine = 'vile/base end,' convien is an impersonal present (convien/conviene) meaning 'it is fitting/necessary,' and che tu caschi uses the subjunctive (caschi) after convien: 'that you should fall'—i.e. 'to what base end must you come!' The construction convien che + subjunctive expresses necessity or inevitability.
segments.paradiso-27-g83: Da l'ora ch'ïo avea guardato prima = 'From the hour that I had looked before.' 'Da' + article + noun gives a temporal starting point; 'ch'' introduces a relative clause ('that/when'). 'ïo avea guardato' uses the imperfect auxiliary avere + past participle guardato to narrate a past action preceding the main action (a literary pluperfect-like sense). 'i' vidi mosso me' places the subject pronoun (i') and the passato remoto vidi ('I saw') before the participle mosso with the direct object me; the sequence means 'I saw myself moved' or 'I saw myself set in motion.' 'per tutto l'arco' = 'throughout the whole arc,' where per = throughout and tutto modifies arco. The relative clause 'che fa dal mezzo al fine il primo clima' describes that arc: 'che' = 'which' and 'fa' = 'makes/constitutes' (fa + complement = 'constitutes from the middle to the end the first clime').
segments.paradiso-27-g84: 'sì ch'io vedea' introduces the result clause 'so that I could see.' 'sì' = 'so/thus' and 'ch'' = 'that.' 'Vedea' is the imperfect (descriptive or vivid past) meaning 'I could see' or 'I saw' repeatedly/continuously. 'di là da Gade' is a locative phrase meaning 'on the other side beyond Gades' (di/da + là combine to mark 'over there beyond'); 'il varco folle d'Ulisse' = 'the mad/rash passage of Ulysses' where folle qualifies varco (folle = reckless/mad).
segments.paradiso-27-g85: The coordinate 'e' = 'and' links the second sight. 'di qua presso il lito' = 'on this side, near the shore' (di qua = on this side; presso = near). 'nel qual' is a relative pronoun ('in which/on which') referring back to the shore. 'si fece Europa dolce carco' uses the reflexive 'si' with fece (literary past of fare or a causative/become sense) to mean 'Europa was made/was rendered a sweet burden' or 'Europa became a sweet load' — an archaic, poetic way to indicate the mythic event; word order (verb after relative) is typical of Dantean syntax.
segments.paradiso-27-g86: 'E più mi fora discoverto il sito di questa aiuola' — 'E' = 'and.' 'Più' = 'more' (further/greater degree). 'Mi fora discoverto' uses the literary conditional/optative 'fora' (would have been) plus the past participle discoverto to form a passive sense: 'would have been revealed to me.' Word order places the indirect object pronoun mi before the verb phrase. 'Il sito di questa aiuola' = 'the site/location of this little flower-bed.' The clause implies 'would have been clearer if not for...' (continued in the next clause).
segments.paradiso-27-g87: 'ma 'l sol procedea sotto i mie' piedi un segno e più partito.' 'Ma' = 'but.' 'L sol' = 'the sun' (archaic form); 'procedea' is imperfect 'was proceeding/moving.' 'Sotto i miei piedi' is literal/astronomical 'under my feet' (i.e., beneath the horizon as Dante moves). 'Un segno e più partito' = 'a sign more distinct/separated'; 'partito' functions adjectivally (past participle used as adjective) meaning 'divided' or 'distinct' — Dante uses it to mean 'clearly separated/marked.' The sentence contrasts what he could have seen with the changing position of the sun.
segments.paradiso-27-g88: Simple noun phrase: 'La mente innamorata' = 'the enamored mind.' 'La' is the article; 'mente' = 'mind'; 'innamorata' an adjective (past participle used adjectivally) meaning 'in love' or 'inflamed by love.'
segments.paradiso-27-g89: 'che donnea con la mia donna sempre' is a relative clause modifying 'la mente innamorata.' 'Che' = 'which/who.' 'Donnea' is an archaic imperfect form of 'donneare' (Dantean verb) meaning roughly 'kept company with/paid court to' or 'acted the lover toward' — here 'was always paying court with my lady.' 'Con la mia donna sempre' = 'always with my lady.'
segments.paradiso-27-g90: 'di ridure ad essa li occhi più che mai ardea' — 'di' introduces the infinitive ridure here (classical Italian uses 'di' before many infinitives). 'Ridure' (infinitive) means 'to bring/turn back' (literally 'lead back') — 'ad essa li occhi' = 'its eyes to her.' 'Più che mai' = 'more than ever' (idiomatic: più + che + mai). 'Ardea' is the imperfect of ardere used figuratively = 'burned' (i.e., was inflamed with desire).
segments.paradiso-27-g91: 'e se natura o arte fé pasture da pigliare occhi' = 'and if nature or art made pasture to catch the eyes.' 'Se' = 'if' (conditional particle). 'Natura o arte' = 'nature or art' as possible sources. 'Fé' (literary past of fare) = 'made/put' and 'pasture' is used metaphorically for 'fodder/attraction' — 'da pigliare occhi' = 'to attract/take the eyes' (i.e., to catch the gaze).' The clause hypothesizes sources of visual attraction.
segments.paradiso-27-g92: 'per aver la mente' = 'in order to have/win the mind.' 'Per' marks purpose; 'aver' is the infinitive 'to have' used transitively here meaning 'to gain' (i.e., to secure the mind/affection); 'la mente' = 'the mind' (object of aver).
segments.paradiso-27-g93: 'in carne umana o ne le sue pitture' = 'in human flesh or in its paintings/depictions.' 'In carne umana' straightforwardly means 'in human flesh' (i.e., actual bodies), 'o ne le sue pitture' = 'or in its pictures/representations' (ne le = in the; sue = its). The phrase contrasts real bodies and artistic depictions as sources of visual delight.
segments.paradiso-27-g94: 'tutte adunate' = 'all gathered together.' 'Tutte' = 'all' (feminine plural agreeing with pitture/occhi implied), 'adunate' = past participle used adjectivally 'gathered.'
segments.paradiso-27-g95: 'parrebber nïente ver' lo piacer divin che mi refulse' — 'parrebber' is conditional plural 'they would seem.' 'Nïente' = 'nothing.' The archaic 'ver'' (short for 'verr' or 'verso') functions as 'toward/compared with' (Dantean usage), so the phrase means 'they would seem nothing compared to the divine pleasure.' 'Lo piacer divin' = 'the divine pleasure' and 'che mi refulse' = 'that shone/reflected on me' (refulse = past tense 'shone upon me').
segments.paradiso-27-g96: 'quando mi volsi al suo viso ridente' = 'when I turned to her smiling face.' 'Quando' = 'when'; 'mi volsi' is passato remoto 'I turned myself' (volgersi/voltarsi) meaning 'I turned (my gaze/body)'; 'al suo viso' = 'to her face'; 'ridente' is a present participle/adjectival form 'smiling/cheerful.'
segments.paradiso-27-g97: 'E la virtù che lo sguardo m'indulse' — 'E' = 'and.' 'La virtù' here means a power/virtue or effective force (not moral virtue) that the look conferred. 'Che' introduces the relative clause 'which the look' and 'm'indulse' is the past form 'indulso/indulse' meaning 'granted/induced to me' (m' = to me). The construction treats a look as imparting a kind of virtù to the speaker.
segments.paradiso-27-g98: 'del bel nido di Leda mi divelse' — 'del bel nido di Leda' = 'from the fair nest of Leda' (mythical allusion). 'Mi divelse' is a poetic past that here means 'tore me away from' or 'separated me from' (divelse used transitively: it removed me). The clause says that the power the look gave pulled him away from the tempting/earthly image evoked by Leda's nest.
segments.paradiso-27-g99: 'e nel ciel velocissimo m'impulse.' 'E' = 'and.' 'Nel ciel velocissimo' = 'into the most swift heaven' (ciel = heaven/sky; velocissimo = very swift). 'M'impulse' is a past-tense form meaning 'impelled me' or 'urged me on' (the verb is transitive with m' = me as object). The clause completes the image: that virtue hurled him upward into the swift heaven.

sentences.paradiso-27-s1: He began, "Glory to the Father, to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit!"
sentences.paradiso-27-s2: — the whole of Paradise, so that the sweet song intoxicated me.
sentences.paradiso-27-s3: That which I saw seemed to me a smile of the universe; therefore my rapture entered through my hearing and through my sight.
sentences.paradiso-27-s6: O life whole in love and in peace!
sentences.paradiso-27-s8: Before my eyes the four torches stood lit; and the one that had come first began to grow more lively, and in its appearance it became such as Jupiter would become, if he and Mars were birds and were to change their feathers.
sentences.paradiso-27-s9: Providence, which here apportions viceregency and office, had placed silence in the blessed choir on every side, when I heard: “If I change color, do not be astonished, for, as I say it, you will see all of these change color.”
sentences.paradiso-27-s10: He who usurps my place on earth — my place, my place, which stands empty in the presence of the Son of God — has made my burial-place a sewer of blood and of stench; wherefore the depraved one who fell from this place up here is appeased there below.”
sentences.paradiso-27-s11: Of that color which, because the sun is opposite, paints the cloud at evening and at morning, I then saw the whole heaven sprinkled.
sentences.paradiso-27-s12: And like an honest woman who remains confident in herself, and, because of others' failing, even while listening becomes timid, so Beatrice changed her appearance; and I believe such an eclipse occurred in heaven when the supreme Power suffered.
sentences.paradiso-27-s33: Thus the skin turns white, then black at the first appearance of the fair daughter of him who brings the morning and leaves the evening.
sentences.paradiso-27-s34: You — so that it may not astonish you — consider that on earth there is no one who governs; hence the human family is so led astray.
sentences.paradiso-27-s35: But before January is completely through its winter—on account of the "centesma" that lies neglected down there—these heavenly circles will shine so that the fortune so long expected will turn the sterns to where the prows are, so that the fleet will sail straight; and true fruit will come after the flower.
sentences.paradiso-27-s17: But the high Providence, which with Scipio defended Rome’s glory in the world, will soon come to the rescue, as I conceive; and you, son, who will yet return down into mortal weight, open your mouth, and do not hide what I do not hide.
sentences.paradiso-27-s18: Just as frozen vapor flakes fall down into our air, when the horn of the goat of heaven touches the sun, so upward I saw the ether thus adorned and flake with triumphant vapours that had made a stay there with us.
sentences.paradiso-27-s19: My face followed her features, and followed until the middle, because of their abundance, the passing of the one in front took them away.
sentences.paradiso-27-s20: So the lady, who saw that I had been freed from waiting upward, said to me: "Now turn your face and see how you are turned."
sentences.paradiso-27-s25: Its parts, most vivid and exalted, are so uniform that I cannot say which Beatrice, by place, chose me.
sentences.paradiso-27-s26: But she, who saw my longing, began — laughing so joyfully that God seemed to rejoice in her face: "The nature of the world, which calms the middle and sets all the rest in motion around it, begins here as though from its limit; and this heaven has no other place than the Divine Mind, in which is kindled the love that turns it and the virtue that he pours down."
sentences.paradiso-27-s27: Light and love of one circle encompass him, just as this [circle] [encompasses] the others; and that girdle only the one who encircles it understands.
sentences.paradiso-27-s28: Its motion is not distinguished by any other thing; but the others are measured by this, just as ten is by the middle and by the fifth; and as time plants its roots in such an order and the branches in the others, it can now be made manifest to you.
sentences.paradiso-27-s29: O covetousness that sinks mortals so far beneath you, that no one has the power to pull their eyes out of your waves!
sentences.paradiso-27-s30: The will blossoms well in men; but continuous rain turns true little shoots into clumsy lumps.
sentences.paradiso-27-s31: Faith and innocence are found only among little children; afterwards each one flees before her cheeks are covered.
sentences.paradiso-27-s32: One such child, still stammering, fasts, who then devours, with his tongue loosened, whatever food under whatever moon; and another, stammering, loves and listens to his mother, who, with full speech, afterwards wished to see her buried.
sentences.paradiso-27-s13: Then her words continued in a voice so altered from herself that her appearance was no longer changed: "The bride of Christ was not brought up from my blood, from Lin’s, from that of Cletus, to be used for the purchase of gold; but for the gaining of this joyful life did Sisto and Pius and Callistus and Urban shed their blood after much lamentation."
sentences.paradiso-27-s14: It was not our intention that on the right hand of our successors part should sit, part on the other side of the Christian people; nor that the keys which were granted to me should become a token on a banner that would fight against the baptized; nor that I should be a figure of a seal for privileges sold and mendacious, wherefore I often blush and quench (the spark).
sentences.paradiso-27-s15: In the garb of a shepherd, rapacious wolves are seen up here throughout all the pastures: O defense of God, why do you still lie (idle)?
sentences.paradiso-27-s16: From our blood the Caorsini and the Guaschi set themselves to drink: O good beginning, to what base end must you fall!
sentences.paradiso-27-s21: From the hour when I had first looked, I saw myself set in motion along the whole arc that makes the first clime from its middle to its end; so that I could see, beyond Gades, Ulysses’ mad passage, and on this side, near the shore where Europa became a sweet burden.
sentences.paradiso-27-s22: And the position of this little flower-bed would have been revealed to me more clearly; but the sun was moving beneath my feet, a sign more distinct.
sentences.paradiso-27-s23: The enamored mind, which ever dallied with my lady, burned more than ever to turn its eyes back to her; and if nature or art made a pasture to take the eyes, in order to win the mind, whether in human flesh or in its paintings, all gathered together would seem nothing compared with the divine pleasure that shone on me when I turned to her smiling face.
sentences.paradiso-27-s24: And the virtue that the glance had bestowed on me tore me from Leda's fair nest, and impelled me into the swiftest heaven.
